channel.title: Feedly Digest
channel.description: Current article list from the Feedly digest on maraz.sk.

item.title: [Reddit Programming] Postmortem: TanStack npm supply-chain compromise
item.description:
TanStack documents an npm compromise caused by privileged GitHub Actions workflow behavior, cache poisoning across trust boundaries, and runtime OIDC token extraction, with dozens of malicious package versions published in minutes.

  • `pull_request_target` remains dangerous when untrusted fork code can influence privileged workflow state.
  • CI cache boundaries are part of the trust model, not just a performance detail.
  • OIDC removes long-lived npm tokens but does not remove the need to protect runtime credential material.
  • Fast detection still leaves a blast radius for hosts that installed compromised versions.

item.title: [Reddit Programming] Mythos finds a curl vulnerability
item.description:
Daniel Stenberg reports that Anthropic's Mythos scan of curl produced one confirmed low-severity vulnerability, several false positives or non-security bugs, and useful bug reports, making it a grounded reality check on AI security tooling.

  • AI scanners helped curl, but human review reduced five claimed findings to one vulnerability.
  • The strongest output was variant hunting and known bug classes, not novel vulnerability discovery.
  • High-quality security reports still require direct source verification before recording a finding.
  • Mature projects benefit less dramatically because fuzzing, SAST, compiler warnings, and review already remove many obvious issues.

item.title: [Reddit Programming] Package Manager CWEs
item.description:
Andrew Nesbitt surveys public CVEs against package managers and extracts recurring failure modes such as archive traversal, symlink handling, registry trust mistakes, and repeated client/server design bugs.

  • Package-manager vulnerabilities repeat across ecosystems because new implementations rediscover old failure modes.
  • CVE counts understate design-level risks like install scripts, maintainer-transfer trust, and first-come namespaces.
  • Archive extraction needs separate handling for `..`, symlinks, platform path separators, and write targets.
  • Security review should be pattern-driven, not ecosystem-specific.

item.title: [Reddit Programming] Be careful with your Git: Investigating malware spreading through Git repositories
item.description:
The article walks through a fake recruiting attack that delivered a Git repo via Google Drive and compromised the developer through malicious Git hooks and obfuscated JavaScript.

  • Git repositories can execute attacker-controlled behavior before a project is ever built or installed.
  • "Review this repo for an interview" is now a realistic developer-targeting malware channel.
  • External repo archives should be treated differently from normal GitHub or GitLab clones.
  • Inspect hooks, config, branches, and scripts before running checkout or build commands from unknown sources.

item.title: [Reddit Programming] How are you effectively interviewing devs now?
item.description:
The r/ExperiencedDevs thread shows teams moving away from take-home assignments toward review-based interviews, live debugging, and candidate accountability for AI-assisted work.

  • Code-review interviews test judgment better than generated take-home output.
  • AI-era interviews should ask candidates to explain defects, tradeoffs, maintainability, and rollout risk.
  • Some teams split interviews into no-AI reasoning checks and AI-allowed build or review tasks.
  • "The tool did it that way" is treated as lack of ownership, not a valid engineering defense.

item.title: [Reddit Programming] How SIMD improved vector search performance in Elasticsearch
item.description:
Elastic explains `simdvec`, the SIMD kernel layer behind Elasticsearch vector distance computations, including AVX-512 and NEON kernels, prefetching, interleaved loads, and quantized-vector support.

  • Vector search performance collapses to repeated distance computation, so kernel-level memory behavior dominates.
  • Separate kernels for `float32`, `int8`, `bfloat16`, binary, and BBQ avoid forcing one representation through one generic path.
  • Prefetching and interleaved loading target the cache-miss path, not just arithmetic throughput.
  • Benchmark data needs to exceed CPU cache and resemble real query workloads before library comparisons mean much.

item.title: [Reddit Programming] Why do some employees intentionally create dependency instead of documenting and sharing knowledge?
item.description:
The discussion reframes knowledge hoarding as a system problem shaped by job-security incentives, undervalued documentation, weak silo management, and automation versus documentation tradeoffs.

  • Knowledge silos persist when firefighting and delivery pressure reward undocumented hero work.
  • Documentation loses value when teammates do not read it or managers do not enforce its use.
  • Automation often beats documentation for recurring operational paths.
  • Managers need explicit silo detection and ownership rotation; blaming only the individual misses the incentive structure.

item.title: [Informatics] Soon We Can Finally Banish JavaScript to the ShadowRealm
item.description:
The article explains TC39's proposed `ShadowRealm` API as an integrity boundary for running JavaScript in isolated global environments without adding new threads.

  • A JavaScript realm is a single-threaded execution environment with its own globals and intrinsics.
  • `ShadowRealm` isolates global scope and built-ins, but it is not a complete security boundary.
  • The proposed API surface is small: `evaluate()` for direct evaluation and `importValue()` for module exports.
  • Current status is pre-browser and useful for mental-model preparation rather than production use.

item.title: [Informatics] Why Keyboard Users Can't Scroll Your Overflow Containers
item.description:
The article shows how overflow containers silently create keyboard-accessibility failures and gives concrete fixes for making scrollable regions reachable and understandable.

  • Scroll containers and keyboard focus are separate browser systems; overflow content can be visible but unreachable by keyboard.
  • `overflow-x: auto` also implies `overflow-y: auto`, creating more scroll behavior than many teams expect.
  • `tabindex="0"` can make a scroll container keyboard-focusable, but it needs an accessible name and visible focus styling.
  • Not every scroll container should enter tab order; unclear labeling is a signal to restructure content.

item.title: [Programming] Migrating Data Ingestion Systems at Meta Scale
item.description:
Meta documents a petabyte-scale MySQL-to-warehouse ingestion migration from customer-owned pipelines to a managed service, with emphasis on shadowing, rollback, and automated validation.

  • Migration success criteria covered data equality, landing latency, and resource utilization before promotion.
  • Reverse shadowing let the new system write production data while the old system remained available for comparison and rollback.
  • Row-count and checksum comparison plus automated mismatch debugging gave engineers concrete failure evidence.
  • CDC rollback needed metadata-level bad-partition marking to stop corrupted deltas from propagating.

item.title: [Engineering] Meet Your Users Where They Are with Obs.js
item.description:
Harry Roberts argues for adapting frontend delivery to real user conditions by exposing browser signals such as latency, bandwidth, battery, CPU, and memory as CSS classes and analytics dimensions.

  • Treat performance as partly site behavior and partly user context; the same code can perform differently across devices and conditions.
  • Separate raw statuses from derived stances so delivery policy remains explicit.
  • Use condition signals for small adaptations: lighter imagery, reduced motion, skipped autoplay, deferred fonts, or simplified checkout.
  • Beaconing capability classes into RUM data can separate code regressions from constrained-user cohorts.

item.title: [Philosophy] Pluralistic: A fascist paradigm (12 May 2026)
item.description:
Cory Doctorow applies Donella Meadows' systems-thinking leverage points to authoritarian politics, arguing that durable change requires contesting system paradigms rather than only tuning laws, rules, or feedback loops.

  • Meadows' leverage hierarchy separates low-level parameter fights from deeper fights over goals, power distribution, and governing assumptions.
  • Dysfunctional systems persist when shared paradigms make bad outcomes feel natural or inevitable.
  • Authoritarian movements can be analyzed as paradigm attacks that weaken belief in collective self-governance before changing institutions.
  • Counter-strategy needs to target the legitimacy of autocratic assumptions, not only individual policies or leaders.

item.title: [Philosophy] Pluralistic: 2024 (apart from the obvious) (11 May 2026)
item.description:
Doctorow argues that compromise-first coalition management failed because it produced low-salience half-measures, muted material wins, and gave voters no affirmative change agenda.

  • A strategy optimized for keeping every faction partly satisfied can produce outcomes that no faction will defend.
  • Policy wins lose value when benefits arrive too late, are too narrow, or are not communicated as proof of power.
  • Institutional blockage requires explicit conflict strategy; quiet procedural work does not mobilize support.
  • Change needs concrete commitments people can recognize, not only opposition to a worse alternative.

item.title: [Psychology] Going Beyond Correlations: Experimental Methods for Investigating the Role of Executive Functions in the Development of Academic Skills
item.description:
The paper surveys experimental methods for studying how executive functions affect academic skills, pushing the field beyond correlational claims toward causal tests.

  • Working memory, inhibitory control, and cognitive flexibility need different experimental manipulation strategies.
  • Adult cognitive methods can be adapted into developmental research instead of relying mainly on observational associations.
  • Working-memory manipulation is the more mature benchmark for judging progress in inhibitory-control and cognitive-flexibility methods.
  • The article is mainly valuable as a methods map for evaluating evidence quality in learning and cognition research.
